items.mistake: Treating logistic regression as regression on continuous targets
items.fix: Use it only for classification: train with BCE, interpret ŷ as P(y=1|x), evaluate with classification metrics (accuracy, precision/recall, AUC).
items.why: The name refers to log-odds linearity, not a continuous y. MSE on labels is the wrong objective.
items.example: Wrong: LogisticRegression on house prices. Right: LogisticRegression on approve/deny with log_loss and AUC.

items.fix: Pass raw logits z to BCEWithLogitsLoss (or logits to frameworks that fuse sigmoid + BCE). Apply sigmoid only for inference probabilities.
items.why: Double sigmoid squashes gradients and produces incorrect loss values near 0 and 1.
items.example: Wrong: loss_fn(torch.sigmoid(logits), y). Right: loss_fn(logits, y) with nn.BCEWithLogitsLoss().

items.mistake: Using accuracy alone on imbalanced data
items.fix: Report precision, recall, F1, and AUC on validation; consider class_weight='balanced' or a threshold tuned on precision-recall curve.
items.why: A majority-class predictor can score high accuracy while failing the minority class — common in fraud and medical screening.
items.example: 99% non-fraud data: always predict 0 gives 99% accuracy but 0% fraud recall.

items.mistake: Assuming the default 0.5 threshold is optimal
items.fix: Choose threshold from validation costs or ROC/PR analysis — e.g. lower threshold when false negatives are expensive.
items.why: BCE optimizes probabilities, not the business-optimal operating point.
items.example: Cancer screen: threshold 0.3 may maximize recall even though training used 0.5 for accuracy demos.
